title: The Macro Minute: What’s the latest regarding US fiscal policy dynamics?

Happy Monday out there, team 42. It's
your skipper here, Darius Dell, to
present our macro minute for Monday,
September 15, 2025. Hope everyone had a
great weekend. So, as always, we'll
start with the executive summary from
today's leadoff morning note. If you
would like to review the analysis
supporting these conclusions and to see
if you need to make any changes in your
portfolio to remain on the right side of
market risk, but to get on the right
side of market risk to the extent you
haven't been following Kiss or Dr. Mo,
need to be a client of 42 macro. So,
today's key macro question is, what's
the latest regarding US fiscal policy
dynamics? Our answer, last week's
release of the August US Treasury
monthly budget statement affords us an
opportunity to check one key driver of
the global economy and global financial
markets, US fiscal policy. On an
annualized calendar year-to-ate basis,
the federal budget deficit has narrowed
$124 billion to 1.9 trillion or 6% of US
GDP. The modest fiscal retrenchment is
being driven primarily by improving
revenue dynamics, which we view as
transitory ahead of anniversarying the
advent of the new tariff regime and the
anticipated easing from the one big ugly
bill in the first half of 2026. To
borrow a phrase from our friend Lyn
Alden, nothing stops this train from a
federal revenues uh federal expenditures
perspective rather, which are up 395
billion and 6% on an annualized calendar
year-to- date year-over-year basis. The
key drivers of growth are number one
Medicare up 159 billion and 17% number
two net interest up 131 billion and 14%
and number three social security up 126
billion and 8%. Each of these categories
is featured in our friend Luke Roman's
true interest expense metric which also
includes national defense. True interest
expense, which accounts for 63% of total
federal expenditures, is up under 386
billion and 9% on an annualized calendar
year-to- date year-over-year basis. The
trailing three-year ker for this metric,
which is almost 2/3 of federal
expenditures and 15% of US GDP, is plus
12%, which is three times the trailing
three-year ker of federal revenues at
plus 4%. The US's internal demographic
and political challenges and extreme
external geopolitical challenges all but
ensures true interest expense will
continue to grow exponentially for the
foreseeable future because there's no
political will to implement structural
reforms. That loud clanking sound you
hear is Congress kicking the proverbial
can down the road. So why does all this
matter? It matters because Uncle Sam is
increasingly crowding out the US and
global economies. Specifically, the
approximate next 12 months marketable
Treasury debt supply of 11.6 trillion
represents 39% of global savings and
215% of US savings, nearly twice the
long run means of the respective time
series. Because Uncle Sam sits at top
the global capital structure, the US
government's historically elevated
borrowing needs means there is hardly
any capital left over to finance the
investment and consumption activity of
the consumers and businesses at the
bottom of the K-shaped US economy. All
told, the PAL Fed's commitment to an
arbitrary 2% inflation target has
contributed to unnecessary hardship
within the US economy. Consistent with
our investing during the forturning
regime framework, we believe the
composition of the FOMC will evolve in a
manner that seeks to address
distributional distortions within the US
economy via structural reforms to how
the Fed sets interest rates and
implements balance sheet policy. These
changes represent substantial right tail
risk for risk assets and gold over a
long-term time horizon while also
representing substantial left tail risk
for the US dollar and Treasury bonds
over that duration. Investors that fail
to adequately position for a structural
regime change at the Fed will likely to
continue dramatically underperforming
those that have. Pay attention to the
forest, which is structural regime
change at the Fed, not the trees, which
is, you know, Trump versus Lisa Cook or
Steve Moran's testimony, hunky humprey
Hawkins rule. Stop paying attention to
the news. Don't watch the news. Pay
attention to the force which is we are
investing during a forturning regime and
the end result is the uh the fiscal
authority is going to you know dominate
the monetary authority and force
financial oppression and monetary
debasement in increasing quantities
because otherwise we're going to wind up
with these distributional problems.
Again forget this is a political cycle.
There are human beings that will go to
the ballot box to choose these outcomes.
So front run that and continue making
money or start making money if you
haven't been participating in KISS or
Dr. M. So transitioning to our 42 micro
dashboard here as always we wrap up with
a question from our community. Uh this
one's really not a a question but is a
really a comment that I think was really
important to share. So uh let me just
read it. Uh love kiss and highly
recommend the macro strategist pro tier
says Darius I just want to say thank you
again. As others have said I found you
via the Adam Tagger via Adam Tagert and

peak prosperity wealthy on days. Adam
Tagard our friend who runs Thoughtful
Money. Um how you doing Adam? uh I had

and had been too conservative and afraid

wanted to be transparent and share that

been amazing. I I was initially using

investing but missed out on some KISS
trades due to their wash rules, timing,
etc. I have since moved all investments
to Fidelity so I was able to quickly
implement KISS pivots. I also upgraded
several months ago to the Macro
Strategist Pro tier. Although I'm just a
retail investor, a few of the daily
trade recommendations have more than
paid for the upgrade and helped me

funds on global Wall Street, of which
many of them are our clients. Again,
we're advising on a cumulative 25 plus
trillion dollars in in their AUM in
terms of our client base. And so, uh,
you know, we're super proud of that,
super proud of the outcomes that KISS
and Dr. Mo continue to deliver to the
